svg.text: For one who knows and sees
Unarisen taints arise and arisen taints increase
anuppannā āsavā uppajjanti, uppannā ca āsavā pavaḍḍhanti
Unarisen taints do not arise; arisen taints are abandoned
anuppannā āsavā na uppajjanti, uppannā ca āsavā pahīyanti
SEVEN METHODS FOR THE ABANDONING OF TAINTS
‘Was I or was I not in the past? How was I in the past?’
‘Will I or will I not be in the future? What and how will I be?’
‘Am I? Am I not? What am I? How am I?’
Fetter of Views Arises and Increases
1. ‘I have a self’ 2. ‘I do not have a self’
3. ‘I perceive the self in itself’ 4. ‘I perceive the self in what is not-self’
5. ‘I perceive what is not-self as the self’ 6. ‘My self is everlasting’
‘This is the way of practice leading to the end of suffering’
doubt · clinging to rules and observances

g.text: Wisely reflecting to guard the six sense faculties
Using robes, food, shelter, and medicine wisely
Enduring cold, heat, hunger, thirst, harsh words
khamo hoti sītassa uṇhassa, jighacchāya pipāsāya
Avoiding wild elephants, bad places, and bad companions
caṇḍaṁ hatthiṁ … agocaraṁ … pāpake mitte parivajjeti
Not tolerating though of sensuality, ill will, or harming
Cultivating the seven factors of awakening
Supported by seclusion, dispassion, based on ending, culminating in complete relinquishment.
“This bhikkhu dwells restrained with regard to all the taints,
has completely cut out craving, unraveled the fetters,
and through full understanding of conceit, has made an end of suffering.”
sabbāsavasaṁvarasaṁvuto viharati, acchecchi taṇhaṁ, vivattayi saṁyojanaṁ, sammā mānābhisamayā antamakāsi dukkhassā
